questions.question: What does QKD provide in a cryptographic system?
questions.explanation: QKD provides secure key distribution—establishing shared secret keys between parties. The actual encryption of messages is done using classical symmetric algorithms (like AES) with the QKD-generated keys.

questions.question: Which type of QKD protocol was introduced first?
questions.explanation: BB84, a prepare-and-measure protocol, was introduced by Bennett and Brassard in 1984—the first QKD protocol. E91 followed in 1991, and continuous-variable protocols came later in 2002.

questions.question: What happens during the 'sifting' phase of QKD?

options.text: Only bits with matching bases are kept

questions.explanation: During sifting, Alice and Bob publicly compare their measurement bases (not the results). They keep only the bits where they used the same basis, discarding approximately half the bits in BB84.

questions.question: What QBER is introduced if Eve intercepts 10% of qubits with an intercept-resend attack?
questions.explanation: When Eve intercepts, she guesses the basis correctly 50% of the time. When wrong (50%), she introduces 50% errors. So: 10% × 50% × 50% = 2.5% QBER. Full interception gives 25% QBER.

questions.question: Which quantum principle ensures eavesdropping is detectable?

options.text: All of the above working together

questions.explanation: QKD security relies on multiple quantum principles: no-cloning (Eve can't copy states), measurement disturbance (measuring changes the state), and Heisenberg uncertainty (wrong basis destroys information). Together, they ensure any eavesdropping attempt is detectable.

questions.question: What is the approximate sifting efficiency of BB84?
questions.explanation: In BB84, Alice and Bob each randomly choose between 2 bases. They match 50% of the time, so approximately half the transmitted bits are discarded during sifting.

questions.question: QKD can work without any classical communication channel.
questions.explanation: QKD requires both a quantum channel (for transmitting qubits) AND an authenticated classical channel (for basis reconciliation, error estimation, and post-processing). The classical channel doesn't need to be secret, but must be authenticated to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.
